usage: To talk about regular actions, facts, and general truths.

forms.positive: Subject + base verb (add -s/-es for he/she/it)
forms.negative: Subject + do/does not + base verb
forms.question: Do/Does + Subject + base verb?

spellingRules: Verbs ending in -o, -sh, -ch add -es.

examples.text: The server runs on Linux.
Does the system require a reboot?

usage: To talk about completed actions in the past.

forms.positive: Subject + past verb
forms.negative: Subject + did not + base verb
forms.question: Did + Subject + base verb?

spellingRules: Regular verbs usually add -ed, but some verbs are irregular.

examples.text: The team fixed the bug yesterday.
Did the deployment fail last night?

usage: To describe actions happening now or around the current time.

forms.positive: Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing
forms.negative: Subject + am/is/are not + verb-ing
forms.question: Am/Is/Are + Subject + verb-ing?

spellingRules: Some verbs drop the final e before adding -ing.

examples.text: The engineers are monitoring the production incident now.
Is the system recovering at the moment?

usage: To describe actions that were in progress at a specific time in the past.

forms.positive: Subject + was/were + verb-ing
forms.negative: Subject + was/were not + verb-ing
forms.question: Was/Were + Subject + verb-ing?

spellingRules: Same -ing spelling rules as present continuous.

examples.text: The on-call engineer was investigating the alert when the outage started.
Were the servers crashing while you were running the migration?

usage: To make predictions, promises, offers, or decisions made at the moment of speaking.

forms.positive: Subject + will + base verb
forms.negative: Subject + will not (won't) + base verb
forms.question: Will + Subject + base verb?

spellingRules: Will does not change form for any subject.

examples.text: We will deploy the fix to production at midnight.
Will the build pipeline finish before the meeting?

usage: To talk about intentions and plans already decided, or predictions based on evidence.

forms.general_formula: Subject + am/is/are + going to + base verb
forms.positive: Subject + am/is/are going to + base verb
forms.negative: Subject + am/is/are not going to + base verb
forms.question: Am/Is/Are + Subject + going to + base verb?

spellingRules: No spelling changes; 'going to' is a fixed phrase.

examples.text: The team is going to refactor the payment module next sprint.
Are you going to attend the planning meeting tomorrow?

usage: To talk about past actions with a present result, or experiences up to now.

forms.positive: Subject + have/has + past participle
forms.negative: Subject + have/has not + past participle
forms.question: Have/Has + Subject + past participle?

spellingRules: Use the past participle form; irregular verbs must be memorised.

examples.text: The team has already merged the pull request.
Have you ever handled a critical production incident?

usage: To describe ongoing actions that started in the past and continue to the present.

forms.positive: Subject + have/has been + verb-ing
forms.negative: Subject + have/has not been + verb-ing
forms.question: Have/Has + Subject + been + verb-ing?

spellingRules: The main verb takes the -ing form.

examples.text: We have been refactoring the authentication module for two weeks.
Has the CI pipeline been failing since the last merge?

usage: To describe an action completed before another past action.

forms.positive: Subject + had + past participle
forms.negative: Subject + had not + past participle
forms.question: Had + Subject + past participle?

spellingRules: Use the past participle; 'had' is the same for all subjects.

examples.text: By the time we responded, the system had already lost ten thousand requests.
Had the team finished testing before the release?

usage: To describe an ongoing past action that happened before another past event.

forms.positive: Subject + had been + verb-ing
forms.negative: Subject + had not been + verb-ing
forms.question: Had + Subject + been + verb-ing?

spellingRules: The main verb takes the -ing form.

examples.text: The attacker had been exploiting the vulnerability for weeks before we detected it.
Had the engineers been monitoring the logs before the breach occurred?

usage: To describe an action that will be completed before a specific future time.

forms.positive: Subject + will have + past participle
forms.negative: Subject + will not have + past participle
forms.question: Will + Subject + have + past participle?

spellingRules: Use the past participle form.

examples.text: By end of Q3, we will have shipped all three milestones.
Will the team have completed the migration before the deadline?

usage: To emphasise the duration of an action that will be ongoing up to a specific future point.

forms.positive: Subject + will have been + verb-ing
forms.negative: Subject + will not have been + verb-ing
forms.question: Will + Subject + have been + verb-ing?

spellingRules: The main verb takes the -ing form.

examples.text: By December, this project will have been running for two years.
Will the server have been running for 90 days straight by the audit date?

usage: To describe general truths, scientific facts, or automatic results.

forms.positive: If you restart the server, it clears the cache.
forms.negative: If the tests don't pass, the build doesn't deploy.
forms.question: What happens if the server runs out of memory?

spellingRules: Both clauses use the present simple tense.

examples.text: If you push to main, the pipeline triggers automatically.
The app crashes if the memory limit is exceeded.

usage: To talk about real, possible future conditions and their likely results.

forms.general_formula: If + present simple, will + base verb
forms.positive: If we test it today, we will release it tomorrow.
forms.negative: If it doesn't work, we won't deploy it.
forms.question: Will we meet the deadline if we start now?

spellingRules: Present simple in the if-clause; will + base verb in the result clause.

examples.text: If we finish the feature today, we will include it in the release.
The team won't hit the deadline unless we add more resources.

usage: To talk about hypothetical or unlikely present/future situations.

forms.general_formula: If + past simple, would + base verb
forms.positive: If I had more time, I would rewrite the whole service.
forms.negative: If we didn't have deadlines, we wouldn't rush.
forms.question: What would you do if the database went down right now?

spellingRules: Past simple in the if-clause; would/could/might + base verb in the result.

examples.text: If we had a bigger team, we would ship features faster.
What would you change if you could rewrite the architecture from scratch?

usage: To talk about hypothetical past situations and their imagined results.

forms.general_formula: If + past perfect, would have + past participle
forms.positive: If we had caught the bug earlier, we would have avoided the outage.
forms.negative: If we hadn't deployed on Friday, it wouldn't have caused problems.
forms.question: Would you have escalated if you had known sooner?

spellingRules: Past perfect in the if-clause; would have + past participle in the result.

examples.text: If the tests had covered that edge case, we would have caught the regression.
The client wouldn't have churned if we had communicated the delay proactively.

usage: To mix time frames — typically a past condition with a present result, or vice versa.

forms.general_formula: If + past perfect, would + base verb (past → present effect)
forms.positive: If we had set up monitoring, we would know about these errors now.
forms.negative: If the system weren't so fragile, we wouldn't have had so many incidents.
forms.question: Would the app be more stable now if we had refactored earlier?

spellingRules: Combine third conditional if-clause with second conditional result.

examples.text: If we had chosen a different cloud provider, we might have lower costs today.
The system would be faster now if we had optimised the queries last year.

usage: Can for present ability or permission; could for past ability, polite requests, or possibility.

forms.positive: I can write unit tests in Jest.
forms.negative: She could not access the admin panel.
forms.question: Could you review my pull request?

spellingRules: Modal verbs do not change form and are always followed by a base verb.

examples.text: I can set up a CI pipeline from scratch.
Could you share your screen during the code review?

usage: Must expresses strong obligation or logical certainty; have to expresses external obligation.

forms.positive: All passwords must be hashed before storage.
forms.negative: You don't have to attend every stand-up.
forms.question: Do we have to write tests for every function?

spellingRules: Must has no past form; use 'had to' for past obligation.

examples.text: Developers must encrypt sensitive data at rest.
Do you have to submit the report by end of day?

usage: To give advice, express expectation, or make a recommendation.

forms.positive: You should write tests before pushing to main.
forms.negative: We shouldn't merge without a review.
forms.question: Should we adopt a stricter code review process?

spellingRules: Both are followed by a base verb without 'to' (except ought to).

examples.text: You should document every public API endpoint.
Shouldn't the CI check run before the PR can be merged?

usage: To express possibility or to give or ask for permission.

forms.positive: The service may experience downtime during the migration.
forms.negative: We might not finish the feature by Friday.
forms.question: May I push this hotfix directly to production?

spellingRules: Both are followed by the base verb; might suggests lower probability.

examples.text: The release might be delayed due to a critical bug.
May I take ownership of this task?

usage: Will for future facts and decisions; would for hypotheticals, polite requests, and reported speech.

forms.positive: The report will show projected growth.
forms.negative: We wouldn't recommend this approach at scale.
forms.question: Would you be available for a sync tomorrow?

spellingRules: Always followed by the base verb.

examples.text: The new infrastructure will reduce latency by 40%.
Would you be able to present the roadmap to the board?

usage: To focus on the action or result rather than who performs it, in the present.

forms.positive: The data is encrypted before storage.
forms.negative: The API is not documented yet.
forms.question: Is the build triggered automatically?

spellingRules: Use the past participle form of the main verb.

examples.text: All API endpoints are documented in the Swagger file.
Is the payment processed through a third-party gateway?

usage: To focus on the action or result in the past, when the agent is unknown or unimportant.

forms.positive: The vulnerability was discovered during a routine audit.
forms.negative: The breach was not detected for several days.
forms.question: Was the system patched before the deadline?

spellingRules: Use the past participle form; was for singular, were for plural.

examples.text: The critical bug was fixed by the on-call team overnight.
Were all users notified after the data breach?

usage: To describe future actions where the focus is on the result, not the actor.

forms.positive: All records will be transferred to the new schema.
forms.negative: Outdated accounts will not be migrated.
forms.question: Will users be notified before the cutover?

spellingRules: Use will be + past participle.

examples.text: The old database will be archived after the migration is complete.
Will downtime be scheduled in advance?

usage: To report what someone said without quoting them directly; verbs shift back in time.

forms.general_formula: Subject + said (that) + clause with backshifted verb
forms.positive: She said the deployment had been successful.
forms.negative: He told us the tests hadn't passed.
forms.question: Did she say when the release would happen?

spellingRules: Present → past; past → past perfect; will → would.

examples.text: The manager said that the deadline had been moved to the following Friday.
She explained that the team had already fixed the critical issue.

usage: To report questions using indirect speech; word order changes to statement order.

forms.positive: She asked if the user had experienced any errors.
forms.negative: He asked why the build had not been triggered.
forms.question: Did the interviewer ask what your biggest technical challenge had been?

spellingRules: No question mark in reported questions; use statement word order.

examples.text: The researcher asked whether users had trouble completing the onboarding flow.
He asked what tools the team had been using for testing.

usage: To identify or define which person, thing, or place you mean; no commas used.

forms.positive: The service that handles authentication is deployed separately.
forms.negative: There is no component that manages this automatically.
forms.question: Which is the module that processes payments?

spellingRules: No comma before the relative clause.

examples.text: The engineer who wrote this module left the company last month.
The API that handles user data must be fully secured.

usage: To add extra information about a noun already identified; commas are required.

forms.positive: The CI/CD pipeline, which runs on every commit, was reviewed by the auditor.
forms.negative: This module, which was not updated, is still causing issues.
forms.question: Is this the new API, which was designed by the senior architect?

spellingRules: Use commas before and after the clause; 'that' is not used here.

examples.text: Our on-call rotation, which covers all time zones, is managed by PagerDuty.
The CTO, who joined last year, approved the new architecture proposal.

usage: A/an for first mention or non-specific nouns; the for specific, known, or unique nouns.

forms.positive: We found a bug in the authentication module.
forms.negative: There is no update available for the system.
forms.question: Is this the right approach for the problem?

spellingRules: Use 'an' before vowel sounds (e.g. an error, an update).

examples.text: I wrote an email to the client explaining the delay.
A new developer joined the team this week.

usage: To compare two things or actions.

forms.positive: This framework is faster than the previous one.
forms.negative: React is not more complex than Angular for simple apps.
forms.question: Is the new API more efficient than the old one?

spellingRules: Short adjectives add -er; longer adjectives use 'more'. Some are irregular (good → better).

examples.text: The new database schema is more scalable than the legacy one.
This solution is cheaper and easier to maintain than the alternatives.

usage: To describe the highest or lowest degree among three or more things.

forms.positive: This is the most efficient algorithm we have written.
forms.negative: That is not the worst solution we have seen.
forms.question: What is the fastest way to reduce build time?

spellingRules: Short adjectives add -est; longer adjectives use 'the most'. Irregular: good → the best.

examples.text: That was the most complex refactoring we had ever done.
Which team member has the most experience with cloud infrastructure?

usage: Gerunds (verb-ing) and infinitives (to + verb) follow certain verbs and prepositions.

forms.general_formula: verb + gerund (enjoy coding) or verb + infinitive (want to deploy)
forms.positive: She wants to propose a new architecture.
forms.negative: He avoids committing directly to main.
forms.question: Do you prefer to work remotely or working in the office?

spellingRules: After prepositions, always use gerunds (e.g. good at testing, instead of writing).

examples.text: The team agreed to implement the new logging strategy.
She suggested using a queue-based approach instead of direct calls.

usage: Separable phrasal verbs can have their object placed between the verb and the particle.

forms.positive: We shut the server down for maintenance.
forms.negative: Don't turn off the alerts during the deployment.
forms.question: Can you set the meeting up for Friday afternoon?

spellingRules: When the object is a pronoun, it must go between verb and particle (shut it down).

examples.text: The DevOps team took the failing service down to apply the patch.
Could you set up the new environment before tomorrow's demo?

usage: Inseparable phrasal verbs keep the verb and particle together; the object always follows.

forms.positive: We need to look into this performance issue.
forms.negative: The new developer has not caught on to the codebase yet.
forms.question: Did you come across any edge cases during testing?

spellingRules: The object always comes after the particle; cannot split the phrasal verb.

examples.text: The security team looked into the suspicious log entries.
We ran into a major compatibility issue after the library upgrade.

usage: Short questions added to the end of a statement to confirm or seek agreement.

forms.general_formula: Positive statement + negative tag? / Negative statement + positive tag?
forms.positive: The specs are finalised, aren't they?
forms.negative: You haven't pushed to production yet, have you?
forms.question: This approach will scale under load, won't it?

spellingRules: The auxiliary verb in the tag matches the main clause; subject becomes a pronoun.

examples.text: The database migration is scheduled for tonight, isn't it?
You've already reviewed this PR, haven't you?

usage: Questions embedded within a statement or another question; use statement word order.

forms.general_formula: Could you tell me + wh-word + statement order?
forms.positive: Could you tell me what the error code means?
forms.negative: I am not sure why the tests are failing.
forms.question: Do you know when the release is scheduled?

spellingRules: No question mark if embedded in a statement; use statement word order after the wh-word.

examples.text: I wonder if you could clarify what the acceptance criteria are.
Could you explain how the caching layer is implemented?

usage: To describe habits, states, or repeated actions in the past that no longer happen.

forms.positive: We used to deploy manually every Friday.
forms.negative: The team didn't use to have automated testing.
forms.question: Did the company use to run on-premise servers?

spellingRules: Negative and question form: 'didn't use to' (no 'd').

examples.text: The application used to store passwords in plain text before the security audit.
Did your team use to do weekly releases before switching to CI/CD?

usage: To describe repeated past actions (not states); often used in storytelling.

forms.positive: Every morning, the team would gather for the standup.
forms.negative: We wouldn't merge on Fridays; it was an unwritten rule.
forms.question: Would the senior developer review every PR personally back then?

spellingRules: 'Would' cannot replace 'used to' with state verbs (know, be, have).

examples.text: In the early days, we would deploy features by copying files manually.
She would always write comprehensive documentation after each feature.

usage: To express regret about the present or past, or a desire for things to be different.

forms.general_formula: I wish / If only + past simple (present wish) or past perfect (past regret)
forms.positive: I wish we had better monitoring tools.
forms.negative: If only the team hadn't deployed on a Friday.
forms.question: Don't you wish the legacy code had been documented properly?

spellingRules: Use past simple for present/future wishes; past perfect for past regrets.

examples.text: I wish we had set up proper alerts before the incident.
If only the stakeholders had communicated the scope change earlier.

usage: To describe an action done by someone else on your behalf.

forms.positive: We had the server audited by an external firm.
forms.negative: We didn't get the design reviewed before launch.
forms.question: Can we get this feature tested by the QA team today?

spellingRules: 'Get' is more informal than 'have'. Both take object + past participle.

examples.text: The company had the entire codebase audited for security vulnerabilities.
Did you get the report reviewed by the compliance team?

usage: To indicate that a service or task is performed by another party for the subject.

forms.positive: We are having the servers upgraded this weekend.
forms.negative: We haven't had the firewall rules reviewed yet.
forms.question: Have you had your application penetration tested recently?

spellingRules: The main verb (have) conjugates; the following verb is always past participle.

examples.text: The start-up had its architecture reviewed by an AWS solution architect.
We should have our API security tested before the product launch.

usage: Words that express quantity or amount before a noun.

forms.positive: We have several open tickets in the backlog.
forms.negative: There are few engineers available this sprint.
forms.question: How many developers are assigned to this task?

spellingRules: 'Many' and 'few' are for countable nouns; 'much' and 'little' for uncountable.

examples.text: There are too many dependencies in this module.
Not much time remains before the release window closes.

usage: Too (excess) and enough (sufficient) to evaluate whether something meets a requirement.

forms.positive: The solution is fast enough to handle peak traffic.
forms.negative: The server is not powerful enough to run this workload.
forms.question: Is the team large enough to deliver this in time?

spellingRules: 'Enough' comes after the adjective but before the noun (enough time, fast enough).

examples.text: The database is too slow to handle real-time queries at this scale.
We don't have enough budget to rewrite the entire backend this quarter.

usage: So (before adjective/adverb) and such (before noun/noun phrase) for emphasis.

forms.positive: It was such a critical bug that we cancelled the release.
forms.negative: The fix was not so straightforward as we had hoped.
forms.question: Was it such a serious issue that you had to escalate to leadership?

spellingRules: Use 'such a/an' before a singular countable noun; 'such' before plurals or uncountable.

examples.text: The outage lasted so long that we had to issue a public statement.
It was such a complex system that only two engineers understood it fully.

usage: To add formality or emphasis by placing a negative adverb or conditional at the start of a clause.

forms.general_formula: Negative/conditional adverb + auxiliary + subject + main verb
forms.positive: Only by automating tests can we guarantee code quality.
forms.negative: Never before have we seen such rapid system growth.
forms.question: Rarely do we encounter a bug of this severity.

spellingRules: Inversion applies to the auxiliary verb; mirrors the question form.

examples.text: Not only did the service fail, but it also caused a cascade of errors.
Rarely do we receive such detailed and actionable user feedback.

usage: To emphasise a specific part of a sentence using 'It is/was...' or 'What...' constructions.

forms.general_formula: It is/was + emphasis + that/who + rest of sentence
forms.positive: It was the misconfigured load balancer that caused the outage.
forms.negative: It wasn't the code changes that broke the build.
forms.question: Was it the network issue that caused the data loss?

spellingRules: Use 'who' for people and 'that' for things in the it-cleft structure.

examples.text: What we really need is a more robust error handling strategy.
It was the missing index that was causing the slow query.

usage: To avoid repetition by omitting words that are understood from context.

forms.general_formula: Omit repeated or understood elements from later clauses
forms.positive: She can write unit tests and so can I.
forms.negative: He didn't update the docs and neither did she.
forms.question: You reviewed the PR, didn't you? — Yes, I did.

spellingRules: Only omit elements that are fully recoverable from context.

examples.text: The back-end team fixed the bug and the front-end team did too.
She wanted to attend the demo but couldn't.

usage: Moving a non-subject element to the front of a sentence for emphasis or cohesion.

forms.general_formula: Fronted element + comma + rest of sentence
forms.positive: This approach, we have tested thoroughly.
forms.negative: The documentation, nobody had updated it for months.
forms.question: The final decision — have you made it yet?

spellingRules: A comma often separates the fronted element from the rest of the sentence.

examples.text: The root cause, we identified it within the first hour.
Security, that is the one area we never compromise on.

usage: Used in formal or hypothetical contexts; 'were' replaces 'was' for all subjects in unreal conditions.

forms.general_formula: It is essential/important that + subject + base verb (present subjunctive); If + subject + were... (past subjunctive)
forms.positive: It is crucial that every developer follow the coding standards.
forms.negative: The committee recommended that the release not proceed without testing.
forms.question: Were the system to fail, what would the recovery procedure be?

spellingRules: In present subjunctive, base verb used for all persons (she go, not she goes).

examples.text: It is vital that the system be patched before the compliance audit.
If the data centre were to lose power, the backup generators would activate.
